qas.q: 9 mission areas of EOD
qas.a:
1. Aerospace Systems/Vehicles and Conventional Munitions
2. Counter-IED
3. Countering WMD
4. Nuclear Weapons Response
5. Unexploded Explosive Ordnance Recovery
6. Operational Range Clearance
7. Defense Support to Civil Authorities
8. Irregular Warfare
9. VIP Protective Support

qas.q: Describe the organization of EOD Flight
qas.a: Be able to sketch the org chart out

qas.q: Describe how CED integrates with the rest of the squadron and the wing
qas.a:
CEN - Site Planning
CEI - Resource Conservation and Recovery
CEO - Engineer Asset Support
CEX - Exercise planning/ Emergency Response
CEF - Emergency Response

qas.q: Summarize the training requirements to enter the EOD career field
qas.a:
1. Higher Fitness Level than rest of AF
2. 3 week Preliminary Course at Sheppard AFB
3. 8 Month school (NAVSCOLEOD) at Eglin AFB

qas.q: EOD AFSC
Explain the mission of CEF and how it fits into the overall mission of a CE squadron
qas.a:
To provide:
1. Fire prevention and protection
2. Fire Fighting
3. Rescue
4. HazMat response capabilities
to prevent and minimize injury, loss of life, and damage to property and environment

qas.q: Describe the function of each CEF element
qas.a: Be able to draw out the structure

qas.q: Describe how CEF integrates with the rest of the squadron and the wing
qas.a:
CEN - Fire code related/ Bed down assistance
CEO - Welding Permits
CEI - Spending Plans
CED - Integrated Response Force
CEX - Emergency Response

qas.q: Fire AFSC
Explain the mission of CEO and how it fits into the overall mission of a CE squadron
qas.a: Effectively & efficiently operate, maintain, and repair Air Force real property and Real Property Installed Equipment (RPIE) through employment of asset management principles

qas.q: Describe the function of each CEO shop or element
qas.a: Be able to draw out structure

qas.q: Describe how CEO integrates with the rest of the squadron
qas.a: Big connection is with Engineer Flight in terms of R&O connecting with Program Optimization as well as with Asset Management in terms of money for maintenance and also Environmental in terms of keeping their ops up to code

qas.q: What 4 Functions does Ops Flight provide
qas.a:
1. Emergency Response Capability
2. Skilled Craft Personnel
3. Management & Oversight
4. Data Analysis/Decision Making

qas.q: 3E0X1
qas.a: Electrical

qas.q: 3EOX2
qas.a: Electrical Power Production

qas.q: 3E1X1
qas.a: HVAC

qas.q: 3E2X1
qas.a: Pavement and Construction Equipment

qas.q: 3E3X1
qas.a: Structural

qas.q: 3E4X1
qas.a: Water and Fuel Systems Maintenance

qas.q: 3E4X3
qas.a: Pest Management

qas.q: 3E5X1
qas.a: Engineering

qas.q: 3E6X1
qas.a: Operations Management

qas.q: Explain the mission of CEN and how it fits into the overall mission of a CE squadron
qas.a:
Handles requirements that exceed Ops Flight Capabilities
1. Comprehensive Planning
2. Program Development
3. Comprehensive Asset Management Plan Integration
4. Design & Project Management
5. Contract Execution
6. Installation mapping & drawing management

qas.q: Describe the function of both CEN elements and their sections
qas.a: Be able to draw out org chart

qas.q: Describe how the CEN integrates with the rest of the squadron
qas.a:
CEF - fire codes for buildings
CEX - facility priority list
CEO - warranty enforcement, utilities optimization,
CEI - Project funding, real property drawings

qas.q: Describe the mission of CEX and how it fits into the overall mission of a CE squadron
qas.a: Enable the Flight, Squadron, and Installation to operate in Contingency environments

qas.q: Describe the function of each CEX element and section
qas.a: be able to draw it out

qas.q: Describe how CEX integrates within the squadron and wing
qas.a:
Squadron - The CEXE element works alot with them on being ready to deploy

Wing - CEXM element works alot with Fire, EOD, and bio environment engineers in med group to be prepared for response missions

qas.q: Describe CEX's role in providing emergency management and CBRN response
qas.a: With the EOC they are the lead for emergency response and coordination with all other emergency functions. They train the wing in CBRN response

qas.q: Readiness & Emergency Management AFSC
Explain the mission of CEI and how it fits into the overall mission of a CE squadron
qas.a: The focus of the Installation Management Flight is to optimize the natural and built real property assets of the installation and support the Squadron through the funding, manpower, and Information Technology (IT) systems.

qas.q: Describe the function of each CEI element
qas.a: draw it out

qas.q: Describe how CEI integrates with the rest of the squadron
qas.a:
Funding is the big one!
real property with CEO and CEN is big
Environmental is related with CEO, CEX, and CEN too

qas.q: Discuss the purpose of Air Force engineers
qas.a: the purpose of Air Force civil engineers is to support the projection of airpower by opening, defending, operating, sustaining, recovering, and when necessary, closing airbases

qas.q: Identify the key factor in the success of the Aviation Engineers in WWII
qas.a: The creation of Aviation Engineers and having a centralized command (9th Engineering Command)

qas.q: Describe the limitations and lessons learned from the SCARWAF program
qas.a: Learned that airmen needed to led airmen. The army was in charge of recruiting, organization, training and equipping while the AF was in charge of funding, but the army had minimal traning and equipment and there good manning went elsewhere first.

qas.q: Describe the rationale behind the creation of Prime BEEF and RED HORSE
qas.a: There was a study group that found that we did not have the capability to have a rapid mobile contingency response. Result was PRIME BEEF with the concept of "peacetime engineers deploy in support of contingencies". We stretched that even further into RED HORSE

qas.q: Describe how the Salty Demo exercise impacted Civil Engineer organization
qas.a:
1. Expeditionary Engineering: General & Geo-spatial engineering performed in an expeditionary environment to beddown and sustain forces.

2. Emergency Services: 3 emergency service flights

qas.q: Explain why the Air Force disagreed with the recommendations of DMRD 967
qas.a: to provide a dedicated, flexible, airfield & base heavy construction & repair capability that is organic, self-sustaining, and has the logistical support to operate independently.

qas.q: Describe the rationale behind the creation of Prime BEEF and RED HORSE
qas.a:
Prime BEEF: smaller scale construction and more focused on maintenance and repair. They establish, sustain, and recover.

RED HORSE: is heavy construction and airfields and focused on being quick and setting up something new. They heavily establish

qas.q: Describe the two core competencies of Prime BEEF
qas.a: For their mission. Prime BEEF has to do mostly the same job CONUS as they will deployed. RED HORSE trains strictly to deploy, so they need to be organized differently.

qas.q: Summarize the RED HORSE mission
qas.a: In garrison Prime BEEF has alot of civilians that carry on the job when military deploys. In a contingency environment, there are much less civilians

qas.q: Differentiate between the capabilities of Prime BEEF & RED HORSE
qas.a: They don't have the emergency services, and they add some other AFSC's in order to be self sustaining. IN garrison it is all military that trains to deploy, and then they do deploy.

qas.q: Explain why Prime BEEF & RED HORSE units are organized differently
qas.a: Doctrine is how you organize and employ. It recommends, not mandates. It is developed by history, debate and analysis, and exercises and contingencies.

qas.q: Describe the employment of Prime BEEF in garrison & contingency operations
qas.a:
1. Installation are power projection platforms
2. Asset Management principles drive how we mitigate risk to installation health
3. We measure readiness by the readiness of the weapon system we support
4. Total Force development at home enables ready airmen engineers to rapidly employ forces and adapt to full spectrum threats.
5. Prime BEEF and RED HORSE supports combatants commanders projection of airpower.

qas.q: Describe the employment of RED HORSE in garrison & contingency operations
qas.a: Prime Beef and RED HORSE

qas.q: Describe what doctrine is and how it is developed
qas.a:
Organizational is from your flight all the way to your MAJCOM. Operational chain of command.

Functional refers to the "AF Civil Engineer Enterprise" this side is where AFCEC is involved and it is more CE leadership for new guidelines on practice and technology and training etc.

qas.q: Explain foundational principles that guide current Air Force Civil Engineer doctrine
qas.a:
NAF (Numbered Air Force) They ensure air sovereignty over certain geographical areas and they fall under a MAJCOM

MAJCOM: A major command that controls a certain Air Force Mission

HAF (Headquarters Air Force): Assists the SECAF and CSAF in running the Air Force

qas.q: Identify two ways we organize, train, equip, and deploy engineer capabilities
qas.a: Normal chain of command to support the overall MAJCOM and the AF mission

qas.q: Distinguish between organization chain of command and functional chain of command
qas.a: It showed we needed all emergency response and base recovery under one command. This lead to CEX and CED joining CE.

qas.q: Describe the roles and structures of each "above wing level" staff organization
qas.a: They both came from lessons learned after 9/11 and Hurricane Katrina

qas.q: Understand how the organizations support your installation mission
qas.a:
Strategic - CAT (Crisis Action Team) lead by the wing commander

Operational - EOC lead by either the MSG/CC or the CE/CC and includes rolls from every support squadron/mission

Tactical - Unit Control Center (UCC) which overseas all the shops in CE

qas.q: Describe how the Salty Demo exercise impacted Civil Engineer organization
qas.a: Back and forth open communication. they direct all tactical CE activities as directed by the EOC.

qas.q: Identify historical examples which led to the establishment of the National Incident Management System (NIMS), and subsequently, the Air Force Incident Management Systems (AFIMS)
qas.a:
Its a way to group services together during emergency ops.
EOD,SecFo,Fire,LRS,FSS,PA etc.

qas.q: Describe the Command and Control (C2) structure of emergency operations within the AFIMS construct at strategic, operational, and tactical levels
qas.a:
EOC Admin
EOC Manager
CE ESF

qas.q: Describe the flow of information between the EOC and UCC
qas.a:
Operator
DART & RADR OIC
UCC OIC
Post-Attack Recon Team

qas.q: Define and list the Emergency Support Functions (ESF)
qas.a:
1. Combat Engineering
2. General Engineering
3. Geospatial Engineering

qas.q: Describe the roles of Civil Engineers within an EOC
qas.a:
Combat: We need to move the combat force, think roads and bridges
General: We need bases
Geo: We need info on terrain, we need maps

qas.q: Describe the roles of Civil Engineers within the CE UCC
qas.a:
Army: has units in all three functions and embedded
Marine: they rely on Navy but have embedded
Navy: They do general and embedded
AF: Embedded and general

qas.q: List the three Joint Engineer Functions
qas.a:
1. The AF fights from its bases, unlike army and navy
2. We did'nt have enough CE to begin with according to war plan requirements
3. Cost effectiveness shouldn't happen at the expense of quality and responsiveness

qas.q: List several common engineer tasks associated with each joint engineer function
qas.a:
Combat: Bridging and earthmoving
General: FOB establishment, electrical
Geo: Maps and Imagery

qas.q: Describe each service's role
qas.a:
Forward Presence
Crisis Response
Naval Mobile Construction Battalion

qas.q: Explain why the Air Force disagreed with the recommendations of DMRD 967
qas.a:
Limited Gen Engineering
Rapid Runway Repair
Can execute OED

qas.q: List common engineer capabilities supporting the Army's mission
qas.a: airmen should be lead by airmen. We got away from that when we started losing our engineers to the army. so we took back control with
